title: Reading and Use of English - Part 2
description: For questions 9 - 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: The aqueducts of ancient Rome are among the most impressive engineering achievements of the ancient world, (0) which stretched for hundreds of kilometres across the Empire. (9) Were it not for the sophisticated surveying methods developed by Roman engineers, such precision over such long distances would have been technically impossible. || (10) It was precisely this combination of mathematical knowledge and practical expertise that set Roman hydraulic engineering apart from earlier civilisations. Scholars continue to debate (11) what motivated such extraordinary public investment in infrastructure, though the need to supply rapidly growing urban populations clearly played a key role. || (12) Not until the nineteenth century did European engineers begin to develop truly comparable systems of water management, and even then they drew heavily on Roman precedents. (13) Had the Romans not mastered the use of hydraulic concrete, entire sections of their aqueducts could never have been built in the way that they were. (14) Such was the ambition of the imperial engineers that several of these structures continue to carry water to this day. (15) Only by examining the individual stones, laid by hand across rugged terrain over many generations, can one fully appreciate the scale of this achievement. (16) No sooner had Rome's engineers resolved one technical problem than another presented itself, driving a continuous cycle of innovation that defined an era.

example.text: The aqueducts of ancient Rome are among the most impressive engineering achievements of the ancient world, (0) which stretched for hundreds of kilometres across the Empire.

questions.explanation: Inversion in a conditional clause without 'if'; 'Were it not for' replaces 'If it were not for', used to express a formal hypothetical condition.
Cleft sentence using 'It was... that' to place emphasis on a specific element; 'it' introduces the cleft structure.
Nominal relative pronoun introducing an indirect question or nominal clause that acts as the object of the verb 'debate'.
Negative fronting structure 'Not until... did', which places the negative adverbial at the start of the sentence and requires subject-auxiliary inversion.
Conditional inversion without 'if' in a third conditional: 'Had the Romans not mastered' replaces 'If the Romans had not mastered'.
Emphatic inversion using 'Such was the [noun] that...', where 'such' is fronted to emphasise the degree or extent of a quality.
Fronted 'Only by [gerund phrase]' requires inversion of subject and auxiliary in the main clause; 'only' initiates the fronted adverbial.
Part of the correlative structure 'No sooner... than', which requires subject-auxiliary inversion after 'No sooner' and expresses rapid succession of events.
